html:
Mikael Olofsson Agricola (Finnish pronunciation: [ˈmikɑel ˈɑɡrikolɑ]), born around 1507 in Torsby, Pernå parish, in eastern Nyland, and died on April 9, 1557, in Nykyrka parish near Viborg, was a reformer of Finland, bishop of the Diocese of Turku from 1554 to 1557, and rector of Åbo School. He is considered the father of the Finnish written language and was an advocate for the Protestant Reformation in Sweden, including Finland during the Swedish era.

== Biography ==

Mikael Agricola was the son of a farmer from Nyland, and his education began in Viborg, at the Viborg Latin School. He became a convert to Protestantism early on, and in 1529 he became secretary to Bishop Martin Skytte in the Diocese of Turku. He studied theology and languages at the University of Wittenberg with both Martin Luther and Philipp Melanchthon from 1536 to 1539. After graduating with a master's degree, Agricola, together with Georg Norman, was hired as a tutor for the princes Erik and Johan (the future Eric XIV and John III respectively) in Stockholm that same summer. However, his stay in Stockholm was brief, and he returned to Turku to become rector of the cathedral school. He remained in this position until 1548, when he took up work as Bishop Skytte's assistant until the bishop's death at the end of 1550. In 1554, he was appointed bishop of the Diocese of Turku, becoming Finland's first Protestant bishop.

== The Father of the Finnish Written Language == Mikael Agricola is often called the father of the Finnish written language because he published the first printed books in Finnish and, through these publications, established a standard for the written language. The first book was Abckiria, an ABC primer, which contained essential elements of Christian belief such as the commandments, the Lord's Prayer, the Apostles' Creed, and more. He was followed by the approximately 900-page prayer book Rucouskiria in 1544, then his translation of the New Testament, Se Wsi Testamenti, in 1548, and the Book of Psalms (1551–1552), all translated into Finnish. He also published Luther's Small Catechism and other books. His writing style was based on Swedish, as Finland was part of Sweden, and Swedish was the dominant language in the country's official life, alongside Low German [source needed] and Latin.

Agricola's written Finnish was based on the dialects of the area around Turku (Åbo). It is debated how he learned Finnish. His hometown was Swedish-speaking, but his mother may have been from the Finnish-speaking inland region. Certain aspects of his language suggest influences from both the inland and Estonian (contacts across the Gulf of Finland were quite active), even though his Finnish primarily draws on the dialects of southwestern Finland. It is clear that he had a talent for languages, which is why his good and idiomatic Finnish may have been acquired later than in his home environment.

== The Treaty of Novgorod == Following Gustav Vasa's war with Russia in 1555–1556, Agricola was sent as part of a delegation to Moscow in February 1557. The delegation also included Archbishop Laurentius Petri, councilors Sten Leijonhuvud and Bengt Gylta, Knut Lillie, and the secretary Olov Larsson, to negotiate peace with Ivan IV Vasiljevitj (Ivan the Terrible). The mission was successful, and a treaty was signed in Novgorod on April 2, 1557. However, Mikael Agricola died on April 9 during his journey home, while riding in a sleigh. Agricola was likely buried inside Viborg Cathedral, but the exact location of his grave is unknown.

== Legacy == Agricola's legacy is that of a reformer of Finland and the father of the Finnish written language or Finnish literature. Mikael Agricola Day is observed annually on April 9 within the Evangelical Lutheran Church. The day is also celebrated as Finnish Language Day, and the Ministry of Education and Culture simultaneously awards prizes for information dissemination. Several foundations and societies are also named after Mikael Agricola, as is the Mikael Agricola Prize, awarded to a deserving translator. The Mikael Agricola Church in Helsinki is named after him. Numerous statues of Agricola are located throughout Finland, including one at the Diocese of Turku, in the Helsinki Cathedral, and formerly in front of the old cathedral in Vyborg (now in Russia). The latter was destroyed during the Winter War, but has been preserved in replicas, such as the one in Lahti. The asteroid 3212 Agricola is named after him.

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well documented in archives. Uncertainty lies more in interpretation and posterity's perception – not least because those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: Mikael Agricola was a significant figure in Finnish history who played a crucial role in spreading Protestant teachings and creating the first written form of the Finnish language.
lattlast:
Mikael Agricola was born around the year 1507. He became a bishop in Turku, which meant he held a high-ranking position within the church. He is very well known as the person who created the Finnish written language because he published the first books in Finnish.

Through his books, he laid the foundation for how to write in Finnish. Since Finland was part of Sweden at that time, he used Swedish extensively as a model when creating the rules for the Finnish written language. He also translated important texts, such as the Bible, into Finnish.

Agricola died in April 1557 during a journey to negotiate peace with Russia. Today, his day is celebrated on April 9th every year. This day is also known as the Day of the Finnish Written Language and is used to celebrate both language and culture.
